justification: Pippen clearly identifies the latency histogram gap and explicitly connects it to the SLA ('you cannot measure your own SLA'), meeting the top rubric criterion. The undifferentiated failure counter is identified — S3 failures lumped into generic requests_failed_total — and a specific recommendation is made (s3_errors_total by error type). However, the recommendation is to break out a separate S3 error metric rather than adding a failure_reason label on the existing failed counter (validation|s3_error|timeout), which is less precise than the rubric expects. The verdict is correctly NOT READY with clear must-fix items. The key miss: file size tracking (upload_file_size_bytes histogram) is listed as must-have #2, a production blocker, when the rubric explicitly states it should be a nice-to-have. This directly matches the failure mode 'recommending file size tracking as a must-have'. The specific histogram recommendation (upload_duration_seconds) is present and correct.

observations.text: Latency histogram gap explicitly identified as blocking SLA monitoring — 'you cannot operate an SLA you cannot measure'
Verdict is correctly NOT READY with clear, prioritized must-fix items
Excellent debugging scenario explaining on-call impact of undifferentiated failure counter
Goes well beyond the rubric with resilience analysis (circuit breaker, memory exhaustion, concurrent uploads)
File size tracking (upload_file_size_bytes histogram) listed as must-have #2 blocker — rubric requires this to be nice-to-have only

Failure counter recommendation is 'break out S3 errors into separate metric' rather than adding a failure_reason label (validation|s3_error|timeout) on the existing counter — less precise than rubric expects and doesn't cover all three failure types in one metric
